title: Tattershall Castle
tags: bar cityofwestminster london pstattershallcastle restaurant river riverthames tattershallcastle thames westminster
description: Westminster | City of Westminster | London | UK Tattershall Castle on the River Thames is a floating pub with a fascinating maritime past. Moored at Victoria Embankment, opposite the London Eye and near Whitehall, this vessel is actually the PS Tattershall Castle, a former Humber ferry built in 1934 by William Gray & Co. in West Hartlepool. Originally serving the Kingston upon Hull–New Holland route, she ferried passengers and goods across the Humber estuary until 1973, even supporting WWII efforts by tethering barrage balloons and transporting troops. After being decommissioned, she was towed to London in 1976, briefly used as an art gallery, and eventually transformed into a pub and restaurant in 1982. Today, she’s a beloved part of London’s riverside scene, offering classic pub fare, comedy nights, and even a nightclub aboard the boat. Her name pays homage to the original Tattershall Castle in Lincolnshire, linking naval heritage with architectural legacy.

title: London - Westminster: Palace of Westminster from St. Stephen's Tavern
tags: london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe westminster cityofwestminster centrallondon palaceofwestminster gothic gothicrevival elizabethtower greatclock bigben clocktower tower augustuspugin ststephenstavern window bar pub
description:
St Stephen’s Tavern, at 10 Bridge Street in Westminster, was originally opened in 1875 and served for decades as a popular watering hole for MPs, journalists, and civil servants due to its location directly across from Parliament. It retains much of its late-Victorian character, including wood-panelled interiors, etched glass, and one of the few remaining division bells wired to alert MPs of a vote.

The Palace of Westminster, seat of the UK Parliament, stands on the north bank of the Thames and largely dates to Charles Barry and Augustus Pugin’s Gothic Revival reconstruction after the 1834 fire. Its layout includes the House of Commons and House of Lords chambers, with key features such as Westminster Hall, dating to 1099, preserved from the medieval palace.  At its northeast corner rises the Elizabeth Tower, designed by Augustus Pugin by completed after his death in 1859, housing the Great Clock and its most famous bell, Big Ben. Standing 96 meters tall, the tower’s four clock faces are each over 7 meters in diameter, making it one of the largest and most accurate public timepieces of its era. Big Ben itself weighs over 13 tons and has become a defining sound and symbol of London.  Originally called simply the Clock Tower, it was renamed in 2012 in honor of Queen Elizabeth II’s Diamond Jubilee.

title: London - Mayfair: Coach & Horses
tags: london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe coachhorses brutonstreet mayfair cityofwestminster pub bar haunted centrallondon westend
description: The Coach & Horses on Bruton Street dates back to the 1770s and is believed to be one of the first properties built on the street. Rebuilt in 1933 for William Younger & Co. after its original structure was damaged and demolished, the pub is notable for its black-and-white timbered façade and stained-glass windows. It is also steeped in legend: in the 18th century, locals told of a ghostly coach drawn by four horses and driven by a headless figure, with eerie passengers peering out from within. Adding to its historic significance, the pub stands near 17 Bruton Street, the birthplace of Queen Elizabeth II in 1926.

title: London - Westminster: Westminster Arms
tags: london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe westminster cityofwestminster centrallondon westminsterarms pub publichouse bar redlion
description: The Westminster Arms, located on Storey’s Gate near Westminster Abbey and the Houses of Parliament, occupies a 19th century building that was rebuilt in 1913.  Originally known as The Red Lion, it is one of only six local public houses fitted with a division bell, summoning MPs and Lords back to Parliament within eight minutes to vote. t offers classic British pub fare and cask ales, spread across multiple floors with a ground-floor bar, a basement cellar bar, and a dining room on the upper level.

title: 1917 at the ivy
tags: uk britain england london victoria theivy ivy artdeco cafe bistro restaurant stgermain bombaysapphire gin alcohol cocktail drink coupe coupeglass lamp mirror
description: Returning from Kensington, we stopped for a drink at a dignified old place called the Ivy in art deco accents on the main Victoria drag. Little did I know what delights awaited.  On its 1917 cocktail menu, so called after the year of its establishment, every drink was some riff on my favorite of liqueurs, St Germain.  There were five lovely options, of which I sampled two:  Marlene Dietrich's Glitz (St Germain, Bombay Sapphire Primer Cru, citric blend, pear syrup, aromatic bitters, peeled cucumber and mint leaves, topped with Prosecco), and the Laurence Olivier Special (St Germain, Bombay Sapphire Primer Cru, Chambord, citric blend, topped with tonic water).  I would have sampled all five, but dinner awaited, and Lonnie would have frowned on a complete tour.

title: London: St. Martin in the Fields
tags: london stmartininthefields crypt cafe
description:
The crypt of St. Martin in the Fields is now a café-restaurant with occasional concerts in the evenings.

St Martin-in-the-Fields is a neoclassical church and was built between 1722 and 1724. The church was designed by James Gibbs, a follower of Christopher Wren. The church is dedicated to Saint Martin.

title: Smithfield.. (and lunch!)
tags: londonambulanceservice smithfieldambulancestation smithfield london south2020 july2020 ambulance mercedessprinter lunchtime lunch cafe sandwich coffee collage bike cagivanavigator1000
description:
Smithfield Ambulance Station..
Called in on my way home to say hi to one of my old three training stations from my time in London Ambulance Service.
We rotated round three stations, Waterloo, Bloomsbury and this one, Smithfield whilst training and post qualification wait to get posted back to the area of London you came from, in my case Wimbledon.
Great memories!
Said hi to a couple crews that came and went whilst I had some lunch at the cafe opposite.

title: City of London, Eastcheap, 33-35
tags: cityoflondon england gradeiistarlistedbuilding london cafe offices wwwenglishtownsnet lbs199434 ebb ebi
description: 1868, by R L Roumieu. Vigorous, Gothic design in red and black brick with painted dressings. 5 storeys plus another in slated roof Enriched, arched doorway plus respond of another opening but rest of ground floor altered for shops. 3 storeys above aranged in 3 bays and run together vertically by shafting, canopies and arcading. 2 arched gables and central, enriched dormer with 2 smaller dormers higher up. Cast-iron cresting. Perhaps one of the most remarkable and dramatic examples of Gothic style applied to a commercial building, it caused a sensation in its day and was widely reported in the building press ( eg 'The Building News' 25 September 1868). EH Listing

description:
Taken at Lady Dinah's #Cat #Emporium. A pretty novel place and an actual cat #cafe where you get to have some #tea and #cakes in the company of #cats. The place is quite a #heaven for cats. Lots of cat #toys including actual #rope #bridges for them to go, #climb, #swing and #sleep upon. This cat was on one such bridge #sleeping and when I pointed my #camera at it to take its picture, it opened it's #eye and looked at me like...who are you and why are you #bothering me, kind of #facial #expression.
This place is located in #Shoreditch, #London.
Taken in a #planned way using my #googlepixel3xl #pixel3xl #googlepixel #Google #pixel phone.

title: 339–343 (odd) Wandsworth Road
tags: wandsworthroad lansdowneway gothic 1843 johndiplock stockwell lambeth london restuarant cafe atoca casabenfica maxcafe
description: Always liked this Castle building and the brick work on building next door above Max Cafe Nos. 339–343 (odd) Wandsworth Road and Nos. 141–149 (odd) Lansdowne Way Formerly Nos. 1, 2 and 3 The Priory This building originally comprised three houses and was built around 1843 (fn. 28) in the Gothic taste of that time. The three-storeyed fronts towards Wandsworth Road and Lansdowne Way are faced with coursed stucco and form a balanced composition centred on the octagonal battlementcrowned bay at the corner, to which the threesided bays terminating each front form responds. The windows have “Gothic” casements and over those to the first floor are moulded labels. Modern shop-fronts have obscured most of the ground storey, but No. 141 retains its original archheaded doorway, framed by a moulded arch and rising from clustered and banded shafts. The property was owned in 1850 by John Cooke; it was purchased in 1866 by John Diplock of Walworth, tea-dealer, whose descendants still retain a partial interest. (fn. 29) www.british-history.ac.uk/survey-london/vol26/pp82-88#h3-...

title: London - Saatchi Gallery
tags: canon eos 6 d 6d ef 16 35 1635 f4 f 4 l is usm voyage travel travelling reise vacation urlaub gb great britain grosbritannien uk united kingdom vereinigtes königreich england london saatchi gallery art museum kunst kunstmuseum
description:
The Saatchi Gallery is a London gallery for contemporary art, opened by Charles Saatchi in 1985 in order to exhibit his collection to the public. It has occupied different premises, first in North London, then the South Bank by the River Thames, and finally in Chelsea, its current location. Saatchi's collection—and hence the gallery's shows—has had distinct phases, starting with US artists and minimalism, moving to the Damien Hirst-led Young British Artists, followed by shows purely of painting, and then returning to contemporary art from America in USA Today at the Royal Academy in London. A 2008 exhibition of contemporary Chinese art formed the inaugural exhibition in the new venue for the gallery at the Duke of York's HQ. 

The gallery has been an influence on art in Britain since its opening. It has also had a history of media controversy, which it has actively courted, and has earned extremes of critical reaction. Many artists shown at the gallery are unknown not only to the general public but also to the commercial art world; showing at the gallery has provided a springboard to launch careers.

In 2010, it was announced that the gallery would be given to the British public, becoming the Museum of Contemporary Art for London.

(Wikipedia)

Die Saatchi Gallery ist eine Galerie für zeitgenössische Kunst in London, die 1985 von Charles Saatchi eröffnet wurde. Der passionierte Kunstliebhaber machte damit seine umfangreiche Sammlung dem breiten Publikum zugänglich.

Von 1985 bis 2002 war die Galerie in einer alten Farbenfabrik in Norden Londons untergebracht, seitdem hat sie zweimal den Standort gewechselt. Im Frühjahr 2003 zog Saatchi mit seiner Sammlung in den früheren Sitz des Londoner Stadtparlamentes, die County Hall an der Themse, die er 2006 nach Uneinigkeiten mit dem japanischen Vermieter wieder aufgab. Am 9. Oktober 2008 wurde die Galerie mit einer Ausstellungsfläche von 50.000 m² im ehemaligen Hauptquartier des Duke of York wieder eröffnet, sie befindet sich in der King’s Road im Stadtteil Chelsea in der Nähe der U-Bahn-Station Sloane Square. Der Eintritt zu allen Ausstellungen ist für die Besucher kostenlos. 

Saatchis Rolle im Kunstbetrieb ist äußerst umstritten. Sie oszilliert zwischen Bewunderung seines künstlerischen Weitblicks und dem Verdacht der gezielten Manipulation vor dem Hintergrund seiner Karriere als Werbemagnat. Zu den angebotenen Künstlern gehören Vertreter der Young British Artists, an deren Etablierung als Gruppe Saatchi nicht unwesentlich mitgewirkt hat[1], wie Jake und Dinos Chapman, Damien Hirst, Tracey Emin, David Batchelor und Jenny Saville, aber auch deutsche Künstler wie Franz Ackermann, Jörg Immendorff, Matthias Weischer, Jonathan Meese, Albert Oehlen und Julian Rosefeldt. Sensation war eine Ausstellung der Young British Artists aus dem Jahre 1997, die aus der Sammlung von Charles Saatchi bestritten wurde und große Aufmerksamkeit erregte. 

Die Galerie wurde in der County Hall jährlich von etwa 600.000 Personen besucht. Sie wurde bis 2006 von 1.000 Schulklassen besichtigt. 

Am 24. Mai 2004 zerstörte ein Feuer viele Werke. Der Schaden wird auf £ 50 Millionen geschätzt.

(Wikipedia)

title: The traffic circle, or roundabout, at Trafalgar Square
tags: trafalgarsquare trafalgar london westminster england art nationalgallery outdoor outdoors people gallery uk unitedkingdom british artgallery square column statue sculpture pedestriancrossing lookleft roundabout trafficcircle pedestrians buses oncomingtraffic winstonchurchillandhisnewyorkmidadventure drivingontheleft
description:
Winston Churchill was struck by a car while crossing the street in New York City and almost killed. Being British, he was accustomed to looking right instead of left for oncoming traffic. Imagine if he had been killed.  That moment in December 1931 was nearly a turning point in world history—quite literally.

Churchill, then 57 and not yet Prime Minister, was in New York City on a lecture tour to recover financial losses from the 1929 crash. One evening, while searching for financier Bernard Baruch’s apartment, Churchill stepped out of a cab in the middle of Fifth Avenue, looked left as he would in London, and began crossing—forgetting that in the U.S., traffic comes from the right.

He was struck by a car driven by Edward F. Cantasano, an unemployed mechanic from Yonkers. The impact fractured Churchill’s ribs and nose and left him with a scalp wound and head contusion. He was rushed to Lenox Hill Hospital, where he later took full responsibility for the accident. Remarkably, Churchill turned the ordeal into a writing opportunity, dictating an article titled “My New York Misadventure” from his hospital bed and selling it for $2,500.

The driver, feeling guilty, called the hospital repeatedly to check on Churchill. Eventually, Churchill invited him to tea at the Waldorf-Astoria and gifted him a signed copy of “The Unknown War.” The incident faded from public memory, but it inspired counterfactual speculation—what if Churchill had died that night? The trajectory of World War II, British leadership, and even the postwar world might have been radically different.

When visiting London, I make sure to look both ways at street crossings.  But it’s nice to see the reminders to "Look Right" on London roads at pedestrian crossings.  Those reminders are a subtle but vital nod to the city’s global visitors.  That “Look Left” instruction at Trafalgar Square is a bit odd, so it makes sense to keep looking both ways, just in case.

[Sources: WinstonChurchill.org, and Wikipedia]

title: National Gallery dome
tags: london city capital uk england britain europe history historic sightseeing iconic trafalgar square national gallery glass dome light opulent
description:
The central hall of the National Gallery boasts a magnificent high ceiling with a glass dome or skylight, which is considered a prominent architectural element.

Architect William Wilkins designed the National Gallery's main building, including the dome, between 1832 and 1838.
The Barry Rooms, part of the extensions designed by Edward Middleton Barry, feature grand spaces with marble pillars and domed ceilings.
Photographs from 1923 show the dome and Rooms 37 and 40, hung with early Italian paintings, including Raphael's "The Ansidei Madonna". 
The glass domes and skylights are a key feature of the National Gallery, contributing to its grand architecture and enhancing the experience for visitors viewing the extensive art collection.

title: Queen's Gallery
tags: uk england london kensingtonpalace queensapartments gallery
description:
Kensington Palace. Queen's State Apartments. The Queen's Gallery. Built in 1693, the Queen's Gallery was once filled with sumptuous artifacts, including Turkish carpets, embroidered silk hangings, and oriental porcelain. It was designed as a light and airy space for Mary to enjoy simple pastimes such as walking, reading and needlework. The portrait at far left is Peter the Great, by Sir Godfrey Kneller. Peter visited London in 1698.

"...Queen Mary's Gallery, a hand-some oak-panelled apartment, 88 ft. in length. ... Among the portraits here are those of Queen Mary and William III., by Kneller."--Baedeker 1915.

IMG_8872

title: an unfortunate choice of name
tags: uk england britain london nationalportraitgallery museum artmuseum artgallery gallery statue brass bronze edwardiii theblackprince edwardtheblackprince richardii king kings plantagenet
description:
We had spent much of Saturday toddling around the soaring galleries of the National Gallery off Trafalgar Square, where the milieu rivals the art for grandeur.  Lonnie, you see, had had it on his short list of museums to visit.  Or so I thought.  After much meandering, I finally persuaded him to ask a docent about the picture of Elizabeth I he wanted to see, and after much confusing discussion, she advised that we would have to "go out and around the corner."  This was confusing, and eventually we puzzled out that we were in the National Gallery, and what he wanted was in the National Portrait Gallery.  Which was on the same site, but a distinct building, nestled in the lee of our museum.  Unfortunate that London decided to use virtually the same name for two museums featuring virtually the same thing (paintings and statuary) and then site them right next to one another.  Predictable, really.

In the National PORTRAIT Gallery, we sped directly to the hall featuring portraits of the Tudors, feasting on the several iconic Elizabeths and Henry VIIIs.  I, however, found somewhat more arresting a trio of Plantagenets--the dynasty that preceded the Tudors, until they conveniently all killed themselves in the War of the Roses between the York and Lancaster sides of the family tree, allowing the marginally-related Henry Tudor to seize the throne as Henry VII.

title: degas degas
tags: uk england britain london nationalgallery museum artmuseum artgallery gallery painting paintings degas impressionism peasantwomen youngspartans
description:
The National Gallery in London is not merely a repository for what appears to be a large potion of the oeuvre of the old masters.  It also has a vast collection of the "new" masters of impressionism and onwards, whom I won't bother to rattle off.  Perhaps fewer, since, being newer, other museums had more of a bite at the apple of such works before Mother England could suck them all into her gravitational maw.  I like the high impressionists and pointillists as much as the next guy, but I have a particular fondness for Degas, and it was there that I found Lonnie, eschewing the medley of Monets and Manets across the room.  

The pair in question are only modestly well known, perhaps -- Peasant Women Bathing in the Sea at Dusk of 1875, and Young Spartans Exercising of 1860.  But I have always thought both are remarkable pieces.  Peasant Women is perhaps a more classically Degas treatment, suggestive and a bit dreamy and in their linked hands in choreographed movement, invoking Degas's more traditional ballet studies whilst entering into new territory of theme and technique.  Young Spartans, on the other hand, is a rather rare subject, but captures the fragility of adolescent psyche and vulnerability to mockery and judgment that will be familiar to anyone who was in high school, as a gaggle of dressed adults stand impassive and oblivious in the background to the boys' and girls' frolicking, fighting or flirting.

description:
If you say that something is more than the sum of its parts or greater than the sum of its parts, you mean that it is better than you would expect from the individual parts, because the way they combine adds a different quality.

According to Aristotle, a philosopher in Ancient Greece, "the whole is greater than the sum of its parts". This observation was adopted to explain human perception by the Gestalt psychology school of thought in the twentieth century.

title: A Fun Place To Explore
tags: streetartgallery leakestreet banksytunnel graffititunnel streetart olivierroubieu mrshiz vibrantcolors street art gallery artwork olivier shiz artists graffiti tunnel colors paintings canvas aerosols spray london uk england explore murals
description:
Leake Street (also known as Banksy Tunnel) in London is where street art or graffiti is legal and promoted despite the fact that it is against UK law on public property.

One of the “Tunnel Rules” is that the art in the tunnel is by its nature live and evolving. It may thus be painted over or modified by other artists. Street artists from all over the world have been drawn to this gallery and each week new “art works” are sprayed over old and the tunnel’s brickwork becomes an ever changing canvas.

The prominent ceiling artwork is by Olivier Roubieu, alias Mr. Shiz, a French artist based in London. I don’t know how long this will stay though.

Explore #04 (2023-11-25) - Thank you for stopping by and for your words of encouragement and favorites!

description: The "Football: Designing the Beautiful Game" at the Design Museum in Kensington, Kensington High Street, London. The exhibition explores the story behind football, unpicking how design has been used to push the game to new limits. It contains 500 objects, films and interviews in sporting performance, kit development, and stadium design and enabling to immerse visitires in the stories of club legacies and game legends including Messi, Pelé, George Best and Diego Maradona. Produced in partnership with the National Football Museum in Manchester, the show reveals the master-planning of the world’s most significant football stadiums, the design innovation used in today’s boots, how the graphic design of team badges, kits and posters shape a club's identity and how grassroots initiatives are pushing back against the sport’s commercialisation. As the world’s most popular sport, football has a truly international dedicated fan base. It is estimated that more than half the population of the planet watched the FIFA World Cup in 2018. Join us for a rare insight into the people and processes that have made football what it is today. Information Source: designmuseum.org/exhibitions/football-designing-the-beaut...

title: View inside Tate Britain
tags: tatebritain gallery art london millbank sonya6400 sonye1018mmf4oss spiralstaircase dxophotolab dxo cs5 interior jimwilson fisheye wideangle
description:
Tate Britain, known from 1897 to 1932 as the National Gallery of British Art and from 1932 to 2000 as the Tate Gallery, is an art museum on Millbank in the City of Westminster. It is part of the Tate network of galleries in England, with Tate Modern, Tate Liverpool and Tate St Ives. It is the oldest gallery in the network, having opened in 1897. It houses a substantial collection of the art of the United Kingdom since Tudor times, and in particular has large holdings of the works of J. M. W. Turner, who bequeathed all his own collection to the nation. It is one of the largest museums in the country.
Info sourced from Wikipedia

It houses a substantial collection of the art of the United Kingdom since Tudor times, and in particular has large holdings of the works of J. M. W. Turner, who bequeathed all his own collection to the nation. It is one of the largest museums in the country.
Info sourced from Wikipedia

title: London - Saatchi Gallery
tags: canon eos 6 d 6d ef 16 35 1635 f4 f 4 l is usm voyage travel travelling reise vacation urlaub gb great britain grosbritannien uk united kingdom vereinigtes königreich england london saatchi gallery art museum kunst kunstmuseum
description:
The Saatchi Gallery is a London gallery for contemporary art, opened by Charles Saatchi in 1985 in order to exhibit his collection to the public. It has occupied different premises, first in North London, then the South Bank by the River Thames, and finally in Chelsea, its current location. Saatchi's collection—and hence the gallery's shows—has had distinct phases, starting with US artists and minimalism, moving to the Damien Hirst-led Young British Artists, followed by shows purely of painting, and then returning to contemporary art from America in USA Today at the Royal Academy in London. A 2008 exhibition of contemporary Chinese art formed the inaugural exhibition in the new venue for the gallery at the Duke of York's HQ. 

The gallery has been an influence on art in Britain since its opening. It has also had a history of media controversy, which it has actively courted, and has earned extremes of critical reaction. Many artists shown at the gallery are unknown not only to the general public but also to the commercial art world; showing at the gallery has provided a springboard to launch careers.

In 2010, it was announced that the gallery would be given to the British public, becoming the Museum of Contemporary Art for London.

(Wikipedia)

Die Saatchi Gallery ist eine Galerie für zeitgenössische Kunst in London, die 1985 von Charles Saatchi eröffnet wurde. Der passionierte Kunstliebhaber machte damit seine umfangreiche Sammlung dem breiten Publikum zugänglich.

Von 1985 bis 2002 war die Galerie in einer alten Farbenfabrik in Norden Londons untergebracht, seitdem hat sie zweimal den Standort gewechselt. Im Frühjahr 2003 zog Saatchi mit seiner Sammlung in den früheren Sitz des Londoner Stadtparlamentes, die County Hall an der Themse, die er 2006 nach Uneinigkeiten mit dem japanischen Vermieter wieder aufgab. Am 9. Oktober 2008 wurde die Galerie mit einer Ausstellungsfläche von 50.000 m² im ehemaligen Hauptquartier des Duke of York wieder eröffnet, sie befindet sich in der King’s Road im Stadtteil Chelsea in der Nähe der U-Bahn-Station Sloane Square. Der Eintritt zu allen Ausstellungen ist für die Besucher kostenlos. 

Saatchis Rolle im Kunstbetrieb ist äußerst umstritten. Sie oszilliert zwischen Bewunderung seines künstlerischen Weitblicks und dem Verdacht der gezielten Manipulation vor dem Hintergrund seiner Karriere als Werbemagnat. Zu den angebotenen Künstlern gehören Vertreter der Young British Artists, an deren Etablierung als Gruppe Saatchi nicht unwesentlich mitgewirkt hat[1], wie Jake und Dinos Chapman, Damien Hirst, Tracey Emin, David Batchelor und Jenny Saville, aber auch deutsche Künstler wie Franz Ackermann, Jörg Immendorff, Matthias Weischer, Jonathan Meese, Albert Oehlen und Julian Rosefeldt. Sensation war eine Ausstellung der Young British Artists aus dem Jahre 1997, die aus der Sammlung von Charles Saatchi bestritten wurde und große Aufmerksamkeit erregte. 

Die Galerie wurde in der County Hall jährlich von etwa 600.000 Personen besucht. Sie wurde bis 2006 von 1.000 Schulklassen besichtigt. 

Am 24. Mai 2004 zerstörte ein Feuer viele Werke. Der Schaden wird auf £ 50 Millionen geschätzt.

(Wikipedia)

title: Bronze statuette of a god with inscribed thigh from Torre Annunziata, 2
tags: museum museo museu musée μουσείο müze artifact ancient antico antique archaeological archeologico archaeology archeologia italy italia campania torreannunziata oplontis pleiades:findspot=432992 bronze bronzo statuette statuetta figura figure god dio zeus giove neptune nettuno inscribed inscription iscrizione iscritta oscan osca falsa false falsum nonsense 3rdcbce iiisecac 2ndhalf3rdcbce campanian campano bronzetto
description: Hellenistic period, ca. 250-200 BCE Said to be from Torre Annunziata (ancient Oplontis; see on Pleiades) The museum plaque reads "HEPILI / LSE.I", offering "Herilios dedicated (this)" as a possible translation, but the editors of Imagines Italicae are of differing opinion: "The text on a bronze statuette of Zeus or Poseidon in the British Museum, allegedly found at Torre Annunziata, acquired by the Sir William Temple, and bequeathed to the British Museum... is in our view a nonsense text" (Imagines Italicae vol. 1, p. 59). title: Bronze coin of *Frentrum with head of Hermes (ImIt *Frentrum 1 Coinage)
tags: museum museo museu musée μουσείο müze artifact ancient antico antique archaeological archeologico archaeology archeologia italy italia campania coin coinage moneta money struck oscan inscription iscrizione osca legend bronze bronzo frentrum samnite sannita mercury mercurio hermes head testa 3rdcbce mid3rdcbce iiisecac samnium sannio cap hat numismatics numismatica
description: Struck bronze coinage Hellenistic period, ca. 275-225 BCE No archaeological provenience; probably struck somewhere in Samnium The sinistroverse inscription in Oscan alphabet and language (Imagines Italicae Frentani / *FRENTRVM 1 Coinage; ST nFr 1; Ve 200, D, 1; HN Italy (2001) no. 621): Text and apparatus Head of Mercury l.; before, right to left, upwards or downwards, frentreí / Pegasus prancing l.; in exergue, right to left frentreí ImIt translation: "At *Frentrum" "The form frentreí, analogous to larinei in the Latin alphabet, ought to be the locative of a city called *Frentrum; but no such place is attested... we wonder whether the settlement on Monte Pallano was called *Frentrum in the pre-Roman period" (ImIt). In the collection of, and photographed on display in, the British Museum, London Lloyd Bequest Inv. CM 1946.1-1.47 / 1946,0101.47 www.britishmuseum.org/collection/object/C_1946-0101-47

title: The Muses by Jacopo Tintoretto (1578) Oil on canvas
tags: themuses muse muses ninemuses music amusement museum jacopo tintoretto london kensingtonpalace fun8 zeus
description: In Greek mythology, Zeus's nine daughters were considered sources of artistic, musical and intellectual inspiration. They became known as the 'muses' and Tintoretto presents them here as strong, dynamic figures in mid performance, lit by the sun god, Apollo. The painting would likely have been hung in a space such as this associated with music, dancing or intellectual debate. The Muses is a 1578 painting by Tintoretto showing the Muses from Greek mythology. It is recorded in the inventory of the collection of Guglielmo Gonzaga, Duke of Mantua in 1627 as hanging alongside Esther Before Ahasuerus in a passage in the Palazzo Ducale in Mantua. It was acquired from the Gonzagas by Charles I of Great Britain. On his execution it was valued at £80 and sold, before being reacquired by the royal family on the Restoration. It now hangs in Kensington Palace. The Royal Collection RCIN 405476 en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Muses_(painting) In ancient Greek religion and mythology, the Muses were the inspirational goddesses of literature, science, and the arts. They were considered the source of the knowledge embodied in the poetry, lyric songs, and myths that were related orally for centuries in ancient Greek culture. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muses Public domain image from Wikimedia Commons w.wiki/FuN8

title: Glass Top
tags: london tate modern art museum
description: My 5000th pic on flickr Tate Modern is an art gallery in London, located in the former Bankside Power Station, in the Bankside area of the London Borough of Southwark. It is one of the largest museums of modern and contemporary art in the world. In April 1994 the Tate Gallery announced that Bankside would be the home for the new Tate Modern. In July of the same year, an international competition was launched to select an architect for the new gallery. Jacques Herzog and Pierre de Meuron of Herzog & de Meuron were announced as the winning architects in January 1995. The £134 million conversion to the Tate Modern started in June 1995 and was completed in January 2000. The most obvious external change was the two-story glass extension on one half of the roof. Much of the original internal structure remained, including the cavernous main turbine hall, which retained the overhead travelling crane. Source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tate_Modern

title: Barts Hospital Museum 6-10-25 5 cupping instruments
tags: nikon z6iii london uk barts bartsnorthwing hospital museum
description:
Case of instruments for cupping, a method of producing slow bleeding as a means of bloodletting. This set includes a scarification to make incisions in the skin, and two types of cups used for different methods of suction. One type could be attached to a valve and syringe to withdraw air when the cup was placed on the skin. The other type could be warmed: as it cooled on the skin, a partial vacuum was created.

title: Door <> Screen
tags: do ho suh tate modern art artist london exhibition museum
description: In ‘The Genesis Exhibition: Do Ho Suh: Walk the House’, Korean-born, London-based artist Do Ho Suh invites visitors to explore his large-scale installations, sculptures, videos and drawings in this major survey exhibition. Is home a place, a feeling, or an idea? Suh asks timely questions about the enigma of home, identity and how we move through and inhabit the world around us. With immersive artworks exploring belonging, collectivity and individuality, connection and disconnection, Suh examines the intricate relationship between architecture, space, the body, and the memories and the moments that make us who we are. Source: www.tate.org.uk/whats-on/tate-modern/the-genesis-exhibiti... Detail within the work Nest/s (2024) Nest/s is connected to an ongoing fabric architecture series Suh calls ‘Hubs’. They form what he describes as an ‘impossible’ architecture. The Hubs serie is based on threshold, such as corridors or entryways, from spaces Suh has occupied throughout his life. In Nest/s, each section is drawn from rooms Suh has inhabited, which together create a continuous passageway. Unlike most building materials, the translucency and delicacy of the textile conveys impressions of the original space rather than acting as a precise replica. The work is made through centuries-old techniques of working with fabric in Korea. The polyester material is today used for traditional Korean summer clothing, emphasizing Suh’s interest in the relationship between architecture and the body. Nest/s breathable and porous quality allows in the surroundings of the museum and the sight of visitors passing through it, blurring the boundaries between inside and outside? Suh considers the fabric architectures to be ‘activated’ once occupied. Source: Info in the exhibition, right next to the work

title: Invisible actually
tags: do ho suh tate modern art artist london exhibition museum dohosuh
description: In ‘The Genesis Exhibition: Do Ho Suh: Walk the House’, Korean-born, London-based artist Do Ho Suh invites visitors to explore his large-scale installations, sculptures, videos and drawings in this major survey exhibition. Is home a place, a feeling, or an idea? Suh asks timely questions about the enigma of home, identity and how we move through and inhabit the world around us. With immersive artworks exploring belonging, collectivity and individuality, connection and disconnection, Suh examines the intricate relationship between architecture, space, the body, and the memories and the moments that make us who we are. Source: www.tate.org.uk/whats-on/tate-modern/the-genesis-exhibiti... Perfect Home: London, Horsham, New York, Berlin, Providence, Seoul (2024) The outline of this work is based on a 1:1 form of the interior of Suh’s present home in London, following his migration here in the 2010s. Inside ‘Perfect Home’ reveals fixtures and appliances from multiple places Suh and his family have inhabited over the years. These light switches, doorknobs and other small elements of our dwelling spaces, touched constantly over time, contribute to our understanding of home. Almost subconsciously, we remember how to reach for these well-used items. Suh color-codes the objects based on their location or origin, and places them at their original height, resulting in a dizzling accumulation of places and time zones within the work. Source: Info in the exhibition, right next to the work

title: 200 anys de ferrocarril!!
tags: 200 years railway science museum london
description: El 27 de setembre de 2025 se celebren els 200 anys del primer ferrocarril modern del món, que va ser al Regne Unit amb la línia Stockton – Darlington. Per celebrar-ho una foto de la locomotora de vapor més antiga del món, la Puffing Billy de 1813/14, que s’exposa al Science Museum de Londres. El 27 de septiembre de 2025 se celebran los 200 años del primer ferrocarril moderno del mundo, que fue en el Reino Unido con la línea Stockton – Darlington. Para celebrarlo, una foto de la locomotora de vapor más antigua del mundo, la Puffing Billy de 1813/14, que se expone en el Science Museum de Londres. On September 27, 2025, the 200th anniversary of the world’s first modern railway will be celebrated, which was in the United Kingdom with the Stockton – Darlington line. title: Invisible actually
tags: do ho suh tate modern art artist london exhibition museum dohosuh
description: In ‘The Genesis Exhibition: Do Ho Suh: Walk the House’, Korean-born, London-based artist Do Ho Suh invites visitors to explore his large-scale installations, sculptures, videos and drawings in this major survey exhibition. Is home a place, a feeling, or an idea? Suh asks timely questions about the enigma of home, identity and how we move through and inhabit the world around us. With immersive artworks exploring belonging, collectivity and individuality, connection and disconnection, Suh examines the intricate relationship between architecture, space, the body, and the memories and the moments that make us who we are. Source: www.tate.org.uk/whats-on/tate-modern/the-genesis-exhibiti... Perfect Home: London, Horsham, New York, Berlin, Providence, Seoul (2024) The outline of this work is based on a 1:1 form of the interior of Suh’s present home in London, following his migration here in the 2010s. Inside ‘Perfect Home’ reveals fixtures and appliances from multiple places Suh and his family have inhabited over the years. These light switches, doorknobs and other small elements of our dwelling spaces, touched constantly over time, contribute to our understanding of home. Almost subconsciously, we remember how to reach for these well-used items. Suh color-codes the objects based on their location or origin, and places them at their original height, resulting in a dizzling accumulation of places and time zones within the work. Source: Info in the exhibition, right next to the work

title: Staircase
tags: do ho suh tate modern art artist london exhibition museum
description: In ‘The Genesis Exhibition: Do Ho Suh: Walk the House’, Korean-born, London-based artist Do Ho Suh invites visitors to explore his large-scale installations, sculptures, videos and drawings in this major survey exhibition. Is home a place, a feeling, or an idea? Suh asks timely questions about the enigma of home, identity and how we move through and inhabit the world around us. With immersive artworks exploring belonging, collectivity and individuality, connection and disconnection, Suh examines the intricate relationship between architecture, space, the body, and the memories and the moments that make us who we are. Source: www.tate.org.uk/whats-on/tate-modern/the-genesis-exhibiti... Staircase (2016) Suh made ‘Staircase’ using a process he developed with gelatine tissue paper, typically used as a support for embroidery and designed to dissolve in water. He sews threads into the tissue paper to create 1:1 scale models of architectural elements and domestic fixtures, such as doors, light switches, sinks, and in this case, a staircase. Suh then places the tissue and threads onto wet sheets of paper, into which the tissue collapses, part-dissolved. Through this fastmoving, collaborative process, three-dimensional space is flattened onto a two-dimensional surface. It evokes the act of folding and packing away, emerging from Suh’s exploration of how we can carry certain spaces with us. Source: Info in the exhibition, right next to the work

title: V&A 5-9-25 8 Cupid kindling the Torch of Hymen
tags: london nikon z6iii va museum
description:
V&A 5-9-25 8 Cupid kindling the Torch of Hymen
George Rennie (1802-60)
Cupid Kindling the Torch of hymen
Signed, about 1831
Cupid, the winged god of love, blows on the torch held by Hymen, the god of marriage, to rekindle the flame of passion. Renniw was a Scottish sculptor and this classical subject is typical of his work. In the 1820s, Rennie studied under the Danish sculptor Bertel Thorvaldsen in Rome, and was heavily influenced by his style, which echoed the sculpture of ancient Greece.

title: V&A 5-9-25 16 Figure of a King 1
tags: london nikon z6iii va museum
description:
V&A 5-9-25 16 Figure of a King 1
Figure of a King
from the Bristol High Cross
About 1400
This figure of a king formed part of the elaborate market cross at Bristol that stood at two different sites in the city before it was dismantled in 1762. In 1764 the merchant banker Henry Hoare bought the cross to embellish the grounds of his estate at Stourhead, Wiltshire, where it remains. This figure and three others were replaced by copies in 1980. The identity of the individual kings is unknown although they may represent John, Henry III, Edward I and Edward III
Limestone with remains of paint and gilding.
carved in England

title: V&A 5-9-25 11 Nita Resch
tags: london nikon z6iii va museum
description:
V&A 5-9-25 11 Nita Resch
Conrad Dressler (1856-1940)
Portrait of Nita Maria Schonfeld Resch (1864-1928)
Nita reach is depicted in this portrait by her husband, Dressler. He made Resch’s features more lifelike by adding colour to the terracotta. Dresser trained at the National Art Training School (later the Royal College of Art) under the French sculpture, Edouard Lanteri. While he gained a reputation as a portrait sculpture early on, Dressler is now best known for his ceramics.

title: V&A 5-9-25 45 Shakespeare
tags: london nikon z6iii va museum
description:
V&A 5-9-25 45 Shakespeare
In 1623 a bookseller, Edward Blount, and a printer, Isaac Haggard, did something remarkable, though they would not have realised at the time. They published a book. this book was Shakespeare’s First Folio, a collection of 26 of William Shakespeare’s plays, 18 of which would have otherwise been lost. The Folio has since taken on iconic status. Now known throughout the world and referenced constantly, it contains a multitude of memorable characters and fascinating plots.
Printed by Isaac Haggard and Edward Blount
Engraving and letterpress with leather binding

title: V&A 5-9-25 8 Cupid kindling the Torch of Hymen
tags: london nikon z6iii va museum
description:
V&A 5-9-25 8 Cupid kindling the Torch of Hymen
George Rennie (1802-60)
Cupid Kindling the Torch of hymen
Signed, about 1831
Cupid, the winged god of love, blows on the torch held by Hymen, the god of marriage, to rekindle the flame of passion. Renniw was a Scottish sculptor and this classical subject is typical of his work. In the 1820s, Rennie studied under the Danish sculptor Bertel Thorvaldsen in Rome, and was heavily influenced by his style, which echoed the sculpture of ancient Greece.

description:
V&A 5-9-25 9 Valour and Cowardice
Alfred Stevens (1817-75)
Valour and Cowardice
1857–66
These are full-size models for two bronze allegorical sculptures for a monument to the Duke of Wellington. Indepted to the powerful figures of Michelangelo, these pieces also influenced the next generation of artists. Stevens devoted much of his career to this monument but sadly did not live to see it installed.

V&A 5-9-25 10 Eugenie Wynne
Aime-Jules Dalou (1838-1902)
Portrait of Eugenie Maria Wynne (1827-99)
Eugenie Wynne moved within the London and Paris art circles, having grown up in both cities. Dalou’s preferred method was modelling in terracotta, which enabled a fluid handling of material evident in the sketch -like rendering of Wynne’s dress, and her sensitively observed features. His approach and teaching later had a major impact on the younger generation of British sculptors.

title: London - Tate Modern: Mark Bradford's Los Moscos
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe markbradford losmoscos mixedmedia abstraction
description:
Los Moscos (2004) by American artist Mark Bradford (born 1961) is a monumental mixed-media canvas created from layered fragments of paper and materials collected near his studio in Inglewood, a historically Black neighborhood of Los Angeles. The work evokes the complex street life of the area, incorporating elements such as posters and brightly colored storefront signage, which Bradford described as painted in “emergency colors” to signal presence and survival.

The title, Spanish for “the flies,” is reappropriated slang sometimes used as a disparaging term for migrant day laborers. Bradford lays out the surface like a sprawling city, with overlapping textures that highlight networks of exchange, tension, and resilience within a single neighborhood. In transforming discarded urban materials into a large-scale abstraction, the work makes visible the socio-political dynamics of community, labor, and identity embedded in the fabric of the city.

Tate Modern, housed in the former Bankside Power Station on the South Bank of the Thames, opened in 2000 as London’s principal museum of modern and contemporary art. The building was originally designed by Sir Giles Gilbert Scott in 1947 and was transformed for museum use by Herzog & de Meuron. Its vast Turbine Hall and multi-level galleries showcase international art from 1900 to the present, spanning movements from Cubism and Surrealism to Abstract Expressionism, Minimalism, Conceptual Art, and contemporary installation.

title: London - Tate Modern: Marcel Duchamp's 3 stoppages étalon
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe marcelduchamp 3stoppagesétalon 3standardstoppages 3stoppagesetalon dada conceptualart
description:
3 stoppages étalon (3 Standard Stoppages) (1913–14, replica 1964) by French artist Marcel Duchamp (1887–1968) is a seminal experiment in chance and measurement. Duchamp dropped three one-metre threads from a height equal to their length, preserving the curved shapes where they landed by cutting wooden rulers to match. He described the result as “a preserved metre, preserved chance,” transforming the rigid standard of measurement into something both arbitrary and poetic.

The work reflects Duchamp’s growing resistance to traditional notions of artistic expression and his fascination with science, parody, and philosophy. He later used the templates in his painting Network of Stoppages (1914) and in preparing The Large Glass (1915–23). The piece also resonates with Henri Poincaré’s scepticism about the absolutes of scientific measurement and with Alfred Jarry’s “pataphysics,” the science of imaginary solutions. Considered by Duchamp as a key turning point in his career, the Stoppages anticipate the conceptual gesture of the readymade and mark his liberation from academic tradition.

Tate Modern, housed in the former Bankside Power Station on the South Bank of the Thames, opened in 2000 as London’s principal museum of modern and contemporary art. The building was originally designed by Sir Giles Gilbert Scott in 1947 and was transformed for museum use by Herzog & de Meuron. title: London - Tate Modern: Max Ernst's Pietà or Revolution by Night
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe pietàorrevolutionbynight pietàoularévolutionlanuit pietaorrevolutionbynight pietaoularevolutionlanuit maxernst surrealism painting
description:
Pietà or Revolution by Night (Pietà ou La révolution la nuit), by German artist Max Ernst, completed in 1923, is an oil painting on canvas in the Tate collection.

In this work, Ernst subverts the Renaissance theme of the Madonna cradling Christ by replacing the Virgin with a figure of his father and the Christ child with himself. The composition reflects Ernst’s engagement with Freudian psychoanalysis, transforming a religious subject into a psychological allegory of family, authority, and trauma. Painted during his early Surrealist years in Paris, it exemplifies the movement’s use of irrational imagery to explore the unconscious. The painting was first owned by the poet Paul Éluard before passing through notable collections and was acquired by Tate in 1981.

Tate Modern, housed in the former Bankside Power Station on the South Bank of the Thames, opened in 2000 as London’s principal museum of modern and contemporary art. title: London - Tate Modern: Pablo Picasso's Seated Woman in a Chemise
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe seatedwomaninachemise femmeenchemiseassise pablopicasso neoclassicism olgakhokhlova painting
description:
Seated Woman in a Chemise (Femme en chemise assise), by Spanish artist Pablo Picasso, completed in 1923, is an oil painting on canvas in the Tate collection.

This portrait depicts Picasso’s wife, ballet dancer Olga Khokhlova, painted in his neoclassical style inspired by Greco-Roman antiquity. The figure’s sculptural form, calm pose, and flowing garment evoke the grandeur of classical sculpture, while also reflecting Khokhlova’s influence in bringing theatrical and performative elements into Picasso’s practice. Radiographic analysis has revealed an underpainting beneath the work, showing Picasso’s reuse of the canvas. Bequeathed by C. Frank Stoop in 1933, the painting has since been exhibited internationally as a key example of Picasso’s post–First World War shift between Cubism and neo-classicism.

Tate Modern, housed in the former Bankside Power Station on the South Bank of the Thames, opened in 2000 as London’s principal museum of modern and contemporary art. title: London - Tate Modern: Max Ernst's Celebes
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe celebes maxernst surrealism dada painting
description:
Celebes, by German artist Max Ernst, was completed in 1921 using oil on canvas.

One of Ernst’s first large-scale paintings, it transforms a Sudanese corn-bin, sourced from an anthropological journal, into a menacing machine-like creature. The title derives from a crude German schoolboy rhyme about elephants, adding absurdity to its dreamlike menace. Juxtapositions such as the elephant-like monster, a headless mannequin, and floating fish recall Freudian free association, embodying Surrealist interest in the unconscious and irrational imagery. Acquired by the Tate in 1975, it previously belonged to poet Paul Éluard and collector Roland Penrose.

title: London - Tate Modern: Man Ray's Cadeau
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe manray cadeau gift flatiron nails iron dada surrealism
description:
Cadeau (Gift), by American artist Man Ray, originally created in 1921 and shown here in an editioned replica from 1972, made of iron and nails, on display at Tate Modern.

First assembled in Paris on the day of his first solo exhibition, Cadeau transforms an ordinary household flat iron into a threatening and eroticized object by embedding a row of nails across its soleplate. The title, meaning “gift” in French, is at odds with its violent impracticality, exemplifying Dada’s subversive humor and Surrealism’s fascination with hidden desires. The original was given to composer Erik Satie but was stolen the same day, surviving mainly through Man Ray’s photographs. Later replicas, including this edition, maintain its status as one of the most iconic Surrealist objects.

Tate Modern, housed in the former Bankside Power Station on the South Bank of the Thames, opened in 2000 as London’s principal museum of modern and contemporary art. title: London - Tate Modern: Natalia Goncharova's Linen
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe linen nataliagoncharova futurism painting
description:
Linen (1913), by Russian avant-garde artist Natalia Goncharova, is an oil on canvas.

The painting depicts the lively activity of a commercial laundry, dividing male garments (shirts, collars, cuffs) from female ones (lace, blouses, aprons). This duality has been read as evoking intimacy between a man and a woman. Interspersed text reflects Goncharova’s collaboration with Russian Futurist and transrational poets, combining words and images in nonlinear, experimental ways. Exhibited in 1913 as Linen (Futurist), the work marks a key phase of Goncharova’s engagement with Futurism and her exploration of modern urban labor through bold form and inscription.

title: London - Tate Modern: Kikuji Yamashita's Deification of a Soldier
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe kikujiyamashita matsurawarerusenshi deificationofasoldier reportagepainting painting surrealism vietnamwar
description:
Deification of a Soldier (Matsurawareru senshi), completed in 1967 by Japanese painter Kikuji Yamashita, is an oil painting on canvas now in the Tate collection.

Created at the height of the Vietnam War, the work depicts a nightmarish vision of violence, filled with screaming mouths, distorted animal forms, and disembodied limbs. At once surreal and chaotic, it captures both Yamashita’s protest against American military intervention in Asia and his own traumatic memories of service in the Imperial Japanese Army during the Second World War, where he was forced to witness and participate in atrocities. The painting belongs to Japan’s “Reportage painting” movement, which sought to confront war and political injustice through graphic and symbolic imagery.

title: London - Tate Modern: Pablo Picasso's Dish of Pears
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe pablopicasso dishofpears compotierdepoires pears stilllife cubism painting
description:
Dish of Pears (Compotier de poires), by Spanish painter Pablo Picasso, completed in 1936 in oil on canvas, on display at Tate Modern.

Painted on 15 December 1936, this still life depicts pears arranged in dishes, rendered in a flat, graphic style. Thick black lines divide the composition into bold shapes, while the white ground was applied last, creating an effect of space behind the fruit rather than a conventional background. The result is a near-abstract pattern of colours and forms, combining elements of naturalistic still life with geometric stylisation. Picasso explored still life subjects across his career, experimenting with variations in form, colour, and abstraction. This work was bequeathed to the Tate in 1983 by Mrs. A.F. Kessler, having earlier passed through the dealers Rosenberg & Helft.

Tate Modern, housed in the former Bankside Power Station on the South Bank of the Thames, opened in 2000 as London’s principal museum of modern and contemporary art. title: London - Tate Modern: Andy Warhol's Marilyn Diptych
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe marilyndiptych andywarhol popart marilynmonroe
description:
Marilyn Diptych, completed in 1962 by American artist Andy Warhol, is executed in alkyd ink, acrylic paint, oil ground, and graphite on two canvases. Measuring over two meters high and nearly three meters wide, it belongs to the Tate collection in London.

The work was made shortly after Marilyn Monroe’s death in August 1962, using a cropped publicity photograph taken by Gene Kornman for the film Niagara (1953). Warhol repeated Monroe’s image fifty times across two panels, the left in vivid colour and the right in stark black and white. This duality has often been read as a commentary on celebrity, mortality, and the fading of public icons. Marilyn Diptych is among the earliest and most celebrated of Warhol’s silkscreen paintings, exemplifying his fusion of mass media imagery and fine art. Originally purchased directly from Warhol’s studio by Burton and Emily Tremaine, the work was later acquired by Tate in 1980.

title: London - Tate Modern: Turbine Hall - Louise Bourgeois' Maman
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe turbinehall maman louisebourgeois sculpture spider surrealism motherhood
description:
Maman, completed in 1999 by French-American artist Louise Bourgeois, is a monumental sculpture in steel on display at the Tate Modern Turbine Hall, Level 1 Bridge.

Towering over nine metres high, the work depicts a spider with eight slender legs supporting a body and an egg sac containing seventeen white and grey marble eggs. Bourgeois created the piece for the opening of Tate Modern in 2000, drawing on the spider as a recurring motif in her art. Both protective and menacing, the spider symbolised her mother, who worked in tapestry restoration—patient, strong, and resourceful, yet also fragile. The title, translating as “Mummy,” underscores these maternal associations while amplifying its unsettling scale and presence. The work was presented to Tate by the artist in 2008.

Tate Modern, housed in the former Bankside Power Station on the South Bank of the Thames, opened in 2000 as London’s principal museum of modern and contemporary art. The building was originally designed by Sir Giles Gilbert Scott in 1947 and was transformed for museum use by Herzog & de Meuron. Its vast Turbine Hall and multi-level galleries showcase international art from 1900 to the present, spanning movements from Cubism and Surrealism to Abstract Expressionism, Minimalism, Conceptual Art, and contemporary installation.

Turbine Hall, a vast industrial space measuring 152 metres long and 35 metres high, originally housed the electricity generators of the former Bankside Power Station  Since Tate Modern opened in 2000, it has been used for the Unilever Series and subsequent large-scale commissions.

title: London - Tate Modern: Marie Laurencin's The Fan
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe thefan marielaurencin l’eventail painting
description:
The Fan (L’Eventail), painted around 1919 by French artist Marie Laurencin, is an oil on canvas.

The work depicts two framed female figures set on a table, one within an oval frame that may represent Laurencin herself, and another whose identity is uncertain—possibly a model or reflection, or even a loved one absent during her exile in Spain during the First World War. Painted in Laurencin’s restrained palette of blues, greys, and soft pinks, the composition suggests intimacy, ambiguity, and female autonomy. The motif connects to a related series of etchings illustrating poems published in 1922 as L’Eventail.

title: Churchill War Rooms
tags: churchillwarrooms cityofwestminster london museum westminster
description: Westminster | City of Westminster | London | UK The Churchill War Rooms are a subterranean time capsule beneath Whitehall, offering a vivid glimpse into Britain’s wartime nerve centre during World War II. Built in 1938 and made fully operational just days before Britain declared war in 1939, this underground complex housed the Cabinet War Rooms where Churchill and his ministers directed the war effort amid the threat of aerial bombardment. Preserved almost exactly as they were left in 1945, the rooms include the Map Room, Transatlantic Telephone Room, and Churchill’s own bedroom, complete with his iconic cigar tray. Now part of the Imperial War Museums, the site also includes the Churchill Museum, which explores the Prime Minister’s life, leadership, and legacy through personal artefacts, interactive exhibits, and archival footage.

Preserved almost exactly as they were left in 1945, the rooms include the Map Room, Transatlantic Telephone Room, and Churchill’s own bedroom, complete with his iconic cigar tray. Now part of the Imperial War Museums, the site also includes the Churchill Museum, which explores the Prime Minister’s life, leadership, and legacy through personal artefacts, interactive exhibits, and archival footage.

title: London - Tate Modern: Vivan Sundaram's May 68
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe vivansundaram may68 popart
description:
May 68 (1968) by Indian artist Vivan Sundaram (1943–2023) reflects the student protests and civil unrest that erupted in Paris during May 1968. Bold colours and geometric abstraction evoke barricades, police helmets, flame guns, and shadowed figures, suggesting a charged street scene. Painted in London, the work shows Sundaram’s engagement with global student activism and protest movements, including those he witnessed in Britain.

Created during his studies at the Slade School of Fine Art, the painting draws on the influence of pop artists David Hockney and Richard Hamilton as well as his tutor R.B. Kitaj. Sundaram employed montage and media-derived imagery to capture the political zeitgeist, while the abstracted bands and flourishes hint at revolutionary symbols such as barricades and the sickle. Though distant from the Paris uprisings, Sundaram aligned himself with the wider energy of anti-apartheid and anti-imperialist struggles, embedding political urgency in his visual language. His later practice, combining installation, photography, and film, continued to confront moments of historical crisis and collective memory.

Tate Modern, housed in the former Bankside Power Station on the South Bank of the Thames, opened in 2000 as London’s principal museum of modern and contemporary art. The building was originally designed by Sir Giles Gilbert Scott in 1947 and was transformed for museum use by Herzog & de Meuron. Its vast Turbine Hall and multi-level galleries showcase international art from 1900 to the present, spanning movements from Cubism and Surrealism to Abstract Expressionism, Minimalism, Conceptual Art, and contemporary installation.

title: London - Tate Modern: Man Ray's Emak Bakia
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe manray emakbakia dada surrealism
description:
Emak Bakia, created in 1926 and remade in 1970 by American artist Man Ray, is composed of a wooden cello neck with loose horsehair attached, mounted on a wooden base.

The object originated from a cello fragment found in a Paris flea market, which Man Ray humorously transformed by giving it flowing white hair, recalling both age and vitality. Its title, meaning “leave me alone” in Basque, comes from an avant-garde film (cinepoem) Man Ray produced in 1926. The original version was featured in that film but later lost. The 1970 reconstruction was made after he rejected a precious silver edition, preferring a return to humble musical materials. This Tate example was presented by the Tate Collectors Forum in 2002.

title: London - Tate Modern: Fernand Léger's Still Life with a Beer Mug
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe fernandléger fernandleger stilllifewithabeermug naturemorteàlachope naturemortealachope beermug stilllife postcubism painting
description:
Still Life with a Beer Mug (Nature morte à la chope), by French painter Fernand Léger, completed in 1921–1922 in oil on canvas, on display at Tate Modern.

Created in the years following the First World War, this still life reflects Léger’s shift away from pure abstraction toward the stylised depiction of ordinary objects, making art more accessible to wider audiences. The composition presents a Bavarian-style beer mug, butter pots, fruit, and a knife set on a table, rendered in bright primary colours and strong contours. These everyday motifs are juxtaposed with a geometric background of diamond and grid patterns in black, white, and grey, framed by a dark blue curtain, creating a dynamic interplay between naturalistic detail and flat abstraction. Evidence of graphite grid lines and reworking indicates Léger’s method of precise planning and refinement, a hallmark of his 1920s practice. The work was acquired for the Tate in 1976 with assistance from the Friends of the Tate Gallery.

Tate Modern, housed in the former Bankside Power Station on the South Bank of the Thames, opened in 2000 as London’s principal museum of modern and contemporary art. title: London - Tate Modern: Mark Rothko's Untitle
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe markrothko untitled abstraction surrealism abstractexpressionism painting
description:
Untitled, by Latvian-born American painter Mark Rothko, completed c.1946–47, oil on canvas, is held in the Tate collection.

This work belongs to Rothko’s transitional period, when he was shifting from myth-inspired Surrealism to the luminous fields of abstraction that would define his mature style. The biomorphic shapes retain a faint resemblance to figures but dissolve into soft layers of lilac, pink, brown, and blue. Rothko argued that by abandoning mythological references he could achieve a more direct “transcendental experience” for the viewer. He described his paintings as dramas, where the forms act as performers embodying the vitality and passion of living organisms.

title: London - Tate Modern: Cildo Meireles' Babel
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe medianetworks babel cildomeireles radios towerofbabel
description:
Babel, by Brazilian artist Cildo Meireles, was completed in two thousand and one using radios, lighting, and sound, and is held in the Tate collection.

The tower is constructed from hundreds of analogue radios, stacked from large wooden valve sets at the base to small mass-produced models at the summit. Each radio is tuned to a different station, producing a constant but shifting mix of voices, music, and static. This cacophony evokes the biblical Tower of Babel as a “tower of incomprehension,” symbolising information overload and the breakdown of communication. Conceived in the early 1990s and first realised at the Kiasma Museum of Contemporary Art in Helsinki, the work was acquired jointly by Tate and the D. Daskalopoulos Collection in 2013.

title: London - Tate Modern: Salvador Dalí's Lobster Telephone
tags: tatemodern museum artmuseum bankside southbank southwark london england britain greatbritain unitedkingdom uk europe salvadordalí salvadordali lobstertelephone téléphonehomard telephonehomard surrealism edwardjames phone telephone lobster
description:
Lobster Telephone (Téléphone - Homard), by Spanish artist Salvador Dalí, completed in 1938, on display at Tate Modern.

This Surrealist object pairs a black Bakelite rotary telephone with a plaster lobster, transforming an everyday communication device into a provocative symbol of desire and absurdity. For Dalí, lobsters and telephones carried erotic associations, and he placed the crustacean’s tail—its sexual organs—directly over the mouthpiece to heighten this effect. The work exemplifies the Surrealist strategy of combining unrelated objects to expose unconscious drives, functioning both as playful invention and as unsettling commentary on food, sex, and communication.

One of several versions commissioned by the English poet and collector Edward James, this example was manufactured with a Belgian Bell telephone base and a Siemens handset. It entered the Tate collection in 1981 after being sold at Christie’s, following its loan to major exhibitions including Dalí’s 1979–80 retrospective at the Centre Pompidou.

title: Headquarters > Workshop > Restaurant
tags: london michelin house workshop garage restaurant bibendum south kensington
description: Michelin House was originally built as the Michelin Tyre Company’s UK headquarters, which opened for business in 1911. The building was specially designed by Michelin employee François Espinasse (1880-1925) and was part of the expansion of the Michelin brand across the Channel. The front of the art deco building was originally a tyre-fitting bay for passing motorists; the building also housed a sales area and a touring office, where motorists could plan their journeys. The architecture of Michelin House reflected the unique and innovative advertising style that Michelin used to promote its brand and products. Stained glass windows, ceramics, ironwork... everything was worked out down to the last detail to attract the attention of customers and this triumphant installation marked the beginning of a long international expansion. In 1985, Michelin fully relocated to Stoke, selling Michelin House to publisher Lord Paul Hamlyn and retailer/restaurateur Sir Terence Conran for £8 million. They subsequently extended the building, restoring many of its original features and creating replicas of the windows and cupolas using drawings and photos. In 1987, Michelin House reopened: a combination of office space, retail outlets – and a restaurant and oyster bar called Bibendum, in homage to the Michelin Man. The chef was Simon Hopkinson and the food was top-notch; it very soon established itself as a fashionable and popular city spot. Source: guide.michelin.com/en/article/features/six-things-you-mig...

Seeing as the London-Brighton run had now departed Hyde Park, and this was a Sunday morning, well before opening time, i took the opportunity to shoot some of the premium brand shops along the way back to Euston.

Situated at the more luxurious Southern  end of Bond Street between  fine jeweller De Grisogono and Chopard.. The new Hublot boutique store, has a floor space of 200 square metres.It replaces the Hublot shop at the Northern end of the street that was run by the watchmaker鈥檚 former UK partner Time Products Luxury, owned by Marcus Margules. 

Hublot isa Swiss luxury watchmaker founded in 1980 by Italian Carlo Crocco. Formerly associated with he Italian Binda Group dynasty, best known for making Breil watches, Carlo Crocco left the company in 1976, to start a watch company. title: 382 Chanel Store, 158-159 New Bond Street, Mayfair, London
tags: chanel chanelstore shop boutique newbondstreet karllangerfield architectpetermarino michaelothoneil
description:
Chanel Store, 158-159 New Bond Street, Mayfair.

Seeing as this was a Sunday morning, well before opening time, i took the opportunity to shoot some of the premium brand shops along the way back to Euston.


The grand new Chanel flagship boutique, opened its doors in June 2013, and at that time was  the largest Chanel shop in the world - is situated at 158-159 New Bond Street, and spans an impressive 12,600 square-feet. The three-storey space - which was 15 months in the making - showcases < The World according to Karl Lagerfield > and houses everything from ready-to-wear, to fine jewellery and beauty. Its interior is the brain child of  architect Peter Marino, who has worked on Chanel boutiques worldwide and houses a sculpture said to be inspired by the strands of Coco Chanel's pearls,  by Jean-Michel Othoniel, and made entirely from hand-blown Venetian glass, as its centre piece. and appears as if suspended from the ceiling, rising from the ground to the top of the third floor. The piece is flanked by the boutique's gravity-defying staircases, which have the appearance of floating due to no visible underlying support. All of the furniture is designed specifically for the store, and all of the art work was commissioned by Marino.

title: venery on portobello road
tags: uk britain england london nottinghill kensington portobelloroad market streetfair openairmarket fur furs shop shopping
description: On Monday morning, it was bright and blue and sunny, and so we embarked on an excursion to the famed open-air street market on Portobello Road in the Notting Hill neighbourhood of Hugh Grant fame.  Our friend from New York Sophia and her sister Bridget joined us, for Lonnie had a very specific target:  a vintage fur, which are far easier to come by in Britain, what with all the estate sales and such.  One of the street shops was an impromptu market for fur, and Lonnie and the girls can be seen swathed among animal hides of various species, considering provenance, quality, animal, color, length, and price sagely.  I didn't feel as though I had much to offer, and there wasn't a lot of space in there, so I took in the pleasant sun and sights of the market.  The shop on the corner, for example, seems to be an old mainstay, with a blazon advertising their boozy wares as the Portobello Road gin distillery for all to see. Yes, I was tempted to give them a try, but opted against, wisely since in short order Lonnie emerged to seek my opnion on his choice.  The specimen was a gorgeous full length mink coat in a dark sepia with highlights of black and, after seeing the very reasonable price for such a lush garment, I gave my hearty approval.  Part Time Fab fan every day.

title: ozymandius, i
tags: uk britain england london knightsbridge harrods escalator escalators relief basrelief ozymandias pillar foyer landing store boutique shop departmentstore hieroglyphics
description:
I wanted to see the glory of one of the classic old department stores of London, and Lonnie wanted to shop.  Thus we were both delighted to go to Harrod's in Knightsbridge.  Lonnie duly secured a proper waistcoast, and I got to marvel at the sheer over-the-topness of their central staircase (now escalators), decorated in mock Egyptian decor.  This landing on the first floor juxtaposed bas reliefs with digital Chanel ads, a strangely felicitous pairing, with stained glass accent and ubiquitous eye-in-the-sky style camera globe complementing.

Perhaps more ominous was the etching on the right over the pharaonic figure, a couplet from Percy Bysshe Shelley's immortal sonnet: "My name is Ozymandias, king of kings / Look on my works, ye Mighty, and despair."  That allusion seemed an odd choice to invoke in the sanctum of a modern temple to concupiscence and avarice.

title: the gluttony department
tags: uk britain england london knightsbridge harrods store departmentstore shop bar restaurant gordonramsayburger drink drinks alcohol cocktails cocktailbar
description:
Nneka had joined Lonnie and me for our afternoon at Harrod's, and after a bout of vigorous shopping and marvelling at the sheer size of their departments, we were feeling distinctly peckish and deserving of a drink. The furniture department alone took up an entire floor and could have accoutred Versailles.  Other departments included cigars, medical services, fine wine, a bookshop, gourmet bakery and greengrocers, jewelry, chronographs, and . . . well, anything else that could be vended.  Really, one need never actually exit Harrod's to obtain any product of modern civilization.

Of course, we naturally expected bar service to be forthcoming.  Alas, for once Harrod's failed.  It was only 14:30, and their premier cocktail bar, the Cabaret, did not open until 15:00 on Tuesdays, evidently. (Obviously, a profound oversight.)  But Harrod's still provided, after a fashion: we were directed back to the fourth floor, where the outpost of Gordon Ramsay Burger offered not only mincemeat delights but select bar service at a more refined counter than I had expected.  The drinks were actually lovely, and the truffled fries even more so.

title: rainbow sixes
tags: uk britain england london westfield mall westfieldmall sixes shop cricket bat cricketbat rainbow hendricks gin
description: We flew overnight on the last day of January to London for the annual midwinter board meeting for Zeta Psi -- for once (though not unexpectedly) Lonnie was joining me.  It was the first time we were holding the board meeting abroad, in recognition of our Oxford chapter's (our first in Europe) fifteenth anniversary.  The hotel was the Hilton Kensington, just across a roundabout from the Westfield, as massive a shopping center there as in America.  Wandering through to pick up a few essentials, we meandered past this gaily-bedizened storefront to things British: a cricket bat served as the signage, and sixes a manner of scoring which I don't pretend to understand.  The advertiser for the tea time? My beloved Hendricks Gin: gin is much bigger in Britain than the US, no doubt thanks to what is practically the national drink, the G&T.

title: WLT 903, London Transport RML 903
tags: aecroutemaster park royal wlt903
description:
WLT 903, London Transport RML 903 (1962 AEC Routemaster / Park Royal) on route 19 at Kings Road, Chelsea.

RML 903 was one of the initial batch of 30 ft long Routemasters which entered service on trolleybus replacement route 104 at Finchley Garage.  By the 1980s, it had returned to Finchley Garage and was adopted as the 鈥榮how bus鈥?  Passing to Holloway Garage, it continued in service with MTL London and Metroline, finally at the Kings Cross outstation for route 390.  It has been retained by Metroline for special events and private hires, and returned to 1960s London Transport livery.

Taken on the route 19 running day organised by the London Bus Museum.
